title: Metric-Dependent Annotation Saturation for Learning from Label Distributions
gist: The number of human annotators needed to capture disagreement signal depends on the evaluation metric: distributional match (KL divergence) saturates at ~10 annotators while entropy correlation needs ~20-50. Soft labels carry item-specific signal that label smoothing cannot replicate, arguing for metric-driven rather than uniform annotation budgets.

insights.claim: In a 3-class NLI setting, distributional match measured by KL divergence saturates by around 10 annotators, reaching 87-95% of its improvement across five model seeds.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Teams optimizing for distributional fidelity can cap annotation spend near 10 labels per item, cutting data-labeling cost.
insights.quote: distributional match (KL divergence) saturates by N ≈ 10

insights.claim: Identifying which items elicit annotator disagreement (entropy correlation) requires roughly 20 to 50 annotators to converge, far more than distributional match.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Disagreement-detection use cases demand 2-5x the annotation budget, a planning input for safety and ambiguity-aware data pipelines.
insights.quote: entropy correlation...requires N ≈ 20–50 annotators to converge

insights.claim: Annotation budgets should be set based on the target evaluation metric rather than applied uniformly across tasks.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Reframes data-labeling procurement as metric-conditional, a lever for ML cost efficiency at scale.
insights.quote: annotation budgets should be informed by the target evaluation metric rather than set uniformly
insights.locator: These results suggest that annotation budgets

insights.claim: Soft labels achieve entropy correlation of r = 0.643 (p < 0.001) versus only r ≈ 0.45-0.49 for label smoothing across five smoothing intensities.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Confirms that collecting real human label distributions captures signal that cheap synthetic smoothing cannot, justifying annotation investment for ambiguity-sensitive models.

insights.claim: Label smoothing cannot distinguish genuinely ambiguous items from clear ones, which is the source of its performance gap versus soft labels.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Explains why uniform regularization fails on ambiguity-heavy tasks like content safety, guiding where to spend on real annotations.
insights.quote: smoothing’s inability to distinguish ambiguous items from clear ones

insights.claim: The soft-label advantage replicates across DeBERTa and RoBERTa architectures, a non-NLI-pretrained baseline, and a cross-domain content safety evaluation.
insights.speaker: Guneet Kohli
insights.so_what: Generalizability across architectures and domains increases confidence the finding will hold in production safety classifiers.
